blogs.title: Sensi: Making Comfort a Design Layer
pullQuotes: But one thing was missing from the stack: how the space will actually feel.
You don't walk into a room and average your experience. You walk in and the thing that's wrong is the thing you notice.
We're not sure where the line is. We built a tool that makes comfort legible, and legibility can become prescription if you're not careful.
Agreement is not truth.
blogs.summary: Sensi is a sensory analysis tool that evaluates architectural floor plans across six senses — thermal, visual, acoustic, spatial, olfactory, and tactile — calibrated to individual user preferences rather than averaged norms. A coupling matrix models how design decisions ripple across sensory dimensions, and the tool runs as a three-act conversational workflow: personalization, iterative plan editing, and rendered output comparison. It was developed by a four-person team during the MaCAD 2025/26 program at IAAC.
blogs.credits: Emilie El Chidiac, Lakzhmy Mari Zaro, María Sánchez Domínguez, Charles Abi Chahine — MaCAD 2025/26, IAAC; faculty: João M. Silva, Bao Trinh, Scott Lebow

blogs.title: The Data Pipeline Behind NeuroSpace: From Sliders to Synapses
pullQuotes: Here is a thought experiment. You are sitting in a room right now.
But what if I told you that every one of those defaults is silently shaping your cortisol levels, your circadian rhythm, and the load on your visual cortex?
And if your building could think about you, it would probably appreciate a few more plants.
Separate compute by latency. Not everything needs the same pipeline.
blogs.summary: NeuroSpace is a browser-based parametric design tool that reframes Building Information Modeling as Behavior Information Modeling: users adjust spatial parameters (ceiling height, wall configuration, natural light, biophilic form, plants) via sliders and receive real-time neurological scores predicting how those choices affect occupant wellbeing. The post details its dual-path data pipeline — server-side Rhino.Compute evaluating a Grasshopper definition for heavy 3D geometry, and client-side JavaScript for instant behavioral scoring — unified through Vue 3/Pinia reactive state and rendered with Three.js, with dashboards exportable via html2canvas and jsPDF.
blogs.credits: Emilie El Chidiac — Master in Advanced Computation for Architecture and Design (MaCAD) 2025/26, IAAC; faculty: Justyna Szychowska, Chuck Driesler, Sophie Moore

blogs.title: The Huddle: Wind-adaptive Research Hub in Punta Arenas, Chile
pullQuotes: Wind, once a problem, ultimately becomes a resource.
Rather than resisting these conditions, the project begins by accepting wind as an omnipresent force.
At any moment, the exact number of parts is known.
The facade becomes a direct, readable map of climatic forces.
blogs.summary: The Huddle is a wind-adaptive research and education hub with an expedition basecamp in Punta Arenas, Chile, one of the world's southernmost cities, defined by a cold, humid, relentlessly windy subpolar climate. Instead of resisting the wind, the project uses WASP-driven computational aggregation, inspired by vernacular Kawesqar (Kawis) huts, to organize modular 4x4x4-meter units according to wind patterns, solar exposure, and program, with Kangaroo solvers shaping the layout and Alpaca4D verifying structural behavior. An adaptive envelope of three panel types (Shields, Lenses, Gills), distributed by a Global Index algorithm, turns the facade into a readable map of climatic forces, while vertical turbines placed in the aggregation's wind tunnels harvest energy from the site's most abundant force.
blogs.credits: Students: María Sánchez Domínguez, Lakzhmy Mari Zaro, Charles Abi Chahine, Emilie El Chidiac — Faculty: Lucia Leva Fuentes, James McBennett, Laukik Lad — Master in Advanced Computation for Architecture and Design (MaCAD 2025-2026), IAAC

blogs.title: Building the Nervous System: How We Turned a Hyper-building Studio Into a Web App
pullQuotes: Santiago, Chile, has an air problem. Not the kind you notice once and forget – the kind that sits in the valley basin like a guest who won't leave.
The app doesn't illustrate the project. It is the project's operating system.
Building a platform alongside the project it supports is messy.
The building breathes. So does the app.
blogs.summary: HB01: The Lungs is a 400-600m hyperbuilding proposal for Santiago, Chile, designed to filter 12 million cubic meters of air annually through parametric, Voronoi-based architecture. The post is told from the Data team's perspective: rather than documenting the studio after the fact, they built a live web platform that tracked KPIs, timelines, team stress, and 3D performance visualizations across three collaborating design teams over the 10-week studio. The app was built with Vue 3, Vite, Tailwind CSS, Speckle for 3D model streaming, driver.js, and Google OAuth.
blogs.credits: Emilie El Chidiac, Maria Sanchez, Lakzhmy Mari Zaro

blogs.title: lEgoarCh: Behind the Sets
pullQuotes: A render is a promise, not a product: you cannot snap a JPEG together on your living-room floor.
The result is connected and 93% supported, just not legible.
The inspired gesture is cheap now; anyone can generate a thousand renders before lunch.
A render is a proposal. A connected, supported, catalog-legal brick model is the proof.
blogs.summary: lEgoarCh converts text prompts into physically buildable LEGO Architecture sets by pairing generative AI for design with deterministic computation for verification. The pipeline generates styled renders with a LoRA-tuned diffusion model, produces 3D meshes, voxelizes the geometry, and optimizes brick placement using real catalog LEGO parts while enforcing structural connectivity, support, and perceptual color accuracy.
blogs.credits: Emilie El Chidiac and Charles Abi Chahine — Faculty: James McBennett and Aymeric Brouez

blogs.title: A Ballooning Market: Why I Decided to Fill a Historic Market with Balloons (And How I Almost Failed)
pullQuotes: Basically, my balloons had no physical awareness of each other. They were just ghosting through one another in a chaotic, colorful mess.
That was the 'I don't care about history' era, where my nice soft balloons were aggressively eating the historic steel columns they were supposed to be 'hugging.'
Either we treat the building like a museum piece that can never be touched, or we drop a cold, glass-and-steel box in the middle of it and call it 'modern.'
For now, the balloons are safely stored in my script (where they can't pop).
blogs.summary: A computational design intervention for Cairo's historic Bab al-Luq market that fills the existing steel structure with inflatable ETFE balloons instead of a conventional glass-and-steel renovation. Built in Rhino/Grasshopper with Kangaroo physics simulation, the balloons pack softly around the heritage columns to create a dual-layer experience: a ground-level souk below and an elevated children's playscape tunnel suspended in the canopy. An RGB color logic in the balloon network doubles as a wayfinding system through the market.
blogs.credits: Emilie El Chidiac — Master in Advanced Computation for Architecture and Design (2025-2026), IAAC; instructors Hesham Shawqy and Angeliki Maragakis

blogs.title: Optimizing For The Mind: Integrating Generative AI and Game Engines into BIM
pullQuotes: But there is no standard, anywhere in the world, that measures whether a building is good for your brain.
A facade is not just an aesthetic choice. It is a public health decision.
We think the field deserves a new name for this data layer. We have been calling it Behavior Information Modeling. Valentine seemed to agree.
Somewhere between the structural engineer's certainty and the neuroscientist's nuance, there is a new kind of architectural practice waiting to emerge.
blogs.summary: A blog post based on an episode of the MaCAD Theory Podcast, hosted by Emilie El Chidiac and Charles Abi Chahine with guest Dr. Cleo Valentine (Cambridge, Harvard, HKS, UCL/RISE), founder of the field of architectural neuroimmunology. It covers her research showing that architectural geometry and biophilic design measurably affect neuroinflammation and stress regulation, her complexity-coherence framework, and the VISTA tool that predicts visual-cortex stress from facade geometry during design. The hosts argue that generative AI, game engines, and computational prediction integrated into BIM workflows can shift the discipline from Building Information Modeling to Behavior Information Modeling, with cognitive performance mandated as a building-validation standard.
blogs.credits: A project of IAAC developed in the Master in Advanced Computation for Architecture and Design (MaCAD) 2025-2026 by Emilie El Chidiac and Charles Abi Chahine, during the course MaCAD 2025/26 BIMSC Theory with Maite Bravo and Hande Karataş. Guest: Dr. Cleo Valentine.

blogs.title: Analyzing Narkomfin Through Its Graph
pullQuotes: The 68% is the model telling you this building doesn't follow the rules.
The building's true spatial units are not rooms or floors — they're vertical slices.
You can see a corridor on a floor plan. But what betweenness shows is that every single path runs through it.
Ginzburg called these units social condensers — architecture meant to reshape how people live together.
blogs.summary: A graph machine learning study of Moscow's Narkomfin Building (Ginzburg & Milinis, 1930), which converts the Type K and Type F duplex units from Rhino OBJ geometry into spatial graphs via grid sampling, ray-casting, and stair connections. Centrality measures, shortest-path analysis, and Louvain community detection reveal that the building's true spatial units are vertical corridor-bound slices rather than rooms or floors. A pretrained GraphSAGE-Pool model trained on Swiss apartments classified Type F rooms at 91.3% accuracy but only 67.9% for Type K — quantifying how deliberately the communal 'social condenser' layout breaks conventional domestic rules.
blogs.credits: Students: Lakzhmy Mari Zaro, María Sánchez Domínguez, Charles Abi Chahine, Emilie El Chidiac — Faculty: Wassim Jabi, Olga Poletkina — MaCAD 2025/26 Graph Machine Learning, IAAC

blogs.title: How a Pasta Shape Became a Pavilion: CAPPELLETTI
pullQuotes: Look closely at a piece of pasta. The curves, ridges, and hollows aren't just for holding sauce; they are feats of structural engineering in miniature.
Welcome to the Cappelletti Pavilion where we decided to scale up dinner and turn pasta into a pavilion.
If a simple pasta shape holds the code for a stable structure, what other everyday objects are hiding the blueprints for the future?
It's a fitting end to a project that so elegantly connects the mathematical with the material, and the digital with the delicious.
blogs.summary: Cappelletti Pavilion translates the mathematical equations describing cappelletti pasta into a full-scale pavilion through computational design. Using evolutionary optimization and finite element analysis in Grasshopper, the team minimized material use to roughly 160 kg while keeping the structure stable, arriving at two distinct lattice topologies for the shell. The final design is proposed in glass-reinforced recycled PET composite at human scale.
blogs.credits: Team member(s): Emilie El Chidiac and Ahmad Baltaji

blogs.title: Encoding Urban Risk: Spatial Feature Analysis and Assessment
pullQuotes: This post documents the full arc of our Urban Safety project — not just the results, but the reasoning, the wrong turns, and what we learned from them.
You can't design collective efficacy, but you can design a street.
Crime is driven by complex social and economic factors that spatial features alone cannot capture.
Being precise about where that uncertainty begins is the most honest contribution this project makes.
blogs.summary: A machine-learning pipeline that classifies street segments from OpenStreetMap into low, medium, and high risk categories based on morphological features such as connectivity, visibility, enclosure, and transit proximity. Trained on roughly 36,000 London street segments grounded in established urban safety theory, the model shows that spatial typologies alone are poor predictors of actual crime, but they do organize streets into coherent morphological groups that transfer across urban contexts. The team wraps the pipeline in a usable assessment interface and is explicit about where the model's certainty ends.
blogs.credits: Students: María Sánchez Domínguez, Charles Abi Chahine, Emilie El Chidiac, Lakzhmy Mari Zaro — Faculty: Gabriella Rossi, Georgios Bekakos (MaCAD, IAAC, 2025-2026)

blogs.title: Computational Bucketing: Turning BIM Data into Visual Insight with Speckle Automate
pullQuotes: In collaborative design projects, reviewing building performance data is often a bottleneck.
In practice, this means opening the Speckle viewer, clicking through elements one by one, and mentally piecing together the bigger picture.
It's slow, and it cuts out anyone without a Speckle account.
A review that previously took 25–30 minutes of manual inspection now takes under three minutes.
blogs.summary: A Speckle Automate function that extracts numeric performance properties from BIM models, groups elements into value-range "buckets" (sized via Scott's and Sturges' rules), and republishes them with gradient color coding. Results feed a live website with an embedded 3D viewer, so collaborators without a Speckle account can spot problem areas spatially. The workflow cuts a manual review from 25-30 minutes to under three minutes.
blogs.credits: Lakzhmy Mari Zaro, María Sánchez Domínguez, Emilie El Chidiac | Faculty: Libny Pacheco, Christoph Berkmiller, Vladimir Ondejcik | Master in Advanced Computation for Architecture and Design (2025-2026)

blogs.title: Turning Data Into Geometry: Rhino.Inside Revit workflows for modelling & documenting
pullQuotes: A central challenge emerged from the nature of the data team itself: if we do not produce geometry, how can our work be read spatially inside the architectural model?
Unlike other teams in the studio, the data team does not generate architectural geometry.
Rather than remaining abstract numbers in spreadsheets, the building's performance indicators become visible components of the architectural model itself.
At this stage, the model becomes both a design artifact and a data visualization tool, ready for export as Revit files, IFC models, or PDF documentation.
blogs.summary: The data team on Hyperbuilding 01, a megastructure project for Santiago de Chile, built parametric Revit families and computational workflows to make building performance metrics — thermal comfort, acoustic impact, air purification — legible as geometry inside the architectural model. Using Rhino.Inside Revit and Grasshopper, they turned abstract KPIs from spreadsheets into visible spatial components, coordinated across studio teams via Speckle, with outputs exportable as Revit files, IFC models, or PDF documentation.
blogs.credits: María Sánchez Domínguez, Lakzhmy Mari Zaro, Emilie El Chidiac; faculty: Óscar Herrero, Arnau Pros, Marina Melguizo

blogs.title: Revitalizing the Tsukiji Fish Market: An Environmental Analysis of Tokyo
pullQuotes: Anyone who has visited Tokyo's famous Tsukiji fish market knows the challenge of dressing for the city's climate.
If it's difficult to simply dress for Tokyo's weather, imagine trying to design a 19-hectare multifunctional venue for it.
Our initial hypothesis was that minor modifications to the original form would suffice, but our analysis proved that these changes had minimal impact on environmental performance.
I recall a trip where my outfits were perpetually wrong for the weather, which swung between 'wet and cold and sometimes hot and humid' in the space of a day.
blogs.summary: A computational environmental analysis of a proposed 19-hectare redevelopment on the site of Tokyo's historic Tsukiji fish market, produced for the MaCAD 25/26 Environmental Analysis course at IAAC. The team simulated thermal comfort, daylight, wind, and heat mitigation under Tokyo's humid subtropical climate (summer temperatures near 32C with humidity above 85%) and iteratively reshaped the building form when minor modifications proved to have minimal impact on environmental performance.
blogs.credits: Students: María Sánchez Domínguez, Charles Abi Chahine, Emilie El Chidiac, Lakzhmy Mari Zaro — Faculty: Angelos Chronis, Aris Vartholomaios, Maria Papadimitraki, Alex Christodoulou, Ekaterina Vititneva, Vasiliki Fragkia, Mohammad Mahdi Mohammadi (MaCAD 25/26 Environmental Analysis)

blogs.title: Pelagñou : An Archipelagic Technoculture
pullQuotes: PART 01. THE INTERNET AS DESIRE PATH
PART 03. DECOLONIAL LINEAGE
PART 04. GLISSANT & OPACITY
PART 06. TENSIONS, AGI & DISCUSSION
blogs.summary: Pelagñou: An Archipelagic Technoculture is a theory project developed by Aditya Kossambe, Emilie El Chidiac, and Eleni Maglari for the MaCAD 2025/26 course "AI in Architecture Theory" at IAAC, taught by Zeynep Aksöz and Mark Balzar. Presented as a sequence of image slides, it traces an archipelagic reading of technoculture across six parts: the internet as desire path, the work of Ari Melenciano, decolonial lineage, Glissant's concept of opacity, the tools involved, and tensions around AGI. The post is tagged Theory and Mixed Reality; all substantive content lives in the slide images rather than body text.
blogs.credits: Aditya Kossambe, Emilie El Chidiac, Eleni Maglari — MaCAD 2025/26, AI in Architecture Theory, with Zeynep Aksöz and Mark Balzar

repos.readmeSummary: Sensi is an architectural layout analysis copilot built with LangGraph (agent), FastAPI (backend), and React + Vite (frontend); it scores sensorial comfort in parametric room layouts, detects conflicts, and suggests design changes while visualizing how modifications to one sense ripple across others.
repos.notable: No live demo, GitHub Pages, or video links found. Best portfolio assets: curated UI screenshots in team_02/docs/shots/ (graph, comfort, topology, galaxy, layout views) and team_02/docs/week08/shots/ (before/after, interactive chat, vision-model report). Two presentation PDFs exist (team_02/docs/week07/Sensi-Presentation-week07.pdf and team_02/docs/week08/Sensi-Presentation-week08.pdf) plus a design system doc at team_02/web/DESIGN_SYSTEM.md. The web app lives at team_02/web (Vite/React, runnable locally). No .mp4/.mov/.gif/.webp assets under team_02.

repos.readmeSummary: The README in her submission folder is only the default Vue 3 + Vite template boilerplate (IDE setup, npm install/dev/build) with no project description.
repos.notable: Repo is PRIVATE (accessed via authenticated gh CLI, not public API). Branch "final" does not exist; Emilie's work lives on branch "final/emilieelchidiac" (a stale "Final/emilieelchidiac" also exists) under src/submissions/EmilieElChidiac/. It is a Vue 3 + Vite + Rhino Compute app called NeuroSpace: a parametric room designer driven by a Grasshopper definition (src/assets/neuro-space.gh) with sliders (Wall Count, Wall Curvature, Ceiling Height, Biophilic Organic Form, Opening Count/Size), drag-and-drop potted plants, a live neuroaesthetic "NeuroScore" HUD (src/utils/neuroScore.js), material/color controls, isometric/perspective modes, and a built-in screenshot capture feature. Zero image/video assets exist in her folder (only public/favicon.ico), no GitHub Pages, no homepage/demo link — portfolio imagery must be produced by running the app locally (npm install; npm run dev in src/submissions/EmilieElChidiac). Key files: src/EmilieElChidiac.vue, src/components/{GeometryView,HeroSection,NeuroScoreHUD,ParameterControls,ResultsPanel}.vue, src/scripts/compute.js, src/styles/neurospace.css.

repos.readmeSummary: lEgoarCh is a generative-AI studio that turns a building description into a buildable LEGO set: FLUX.2 renders the architecture, TRELLIS-2 generates a 3D mesh, and a custom "legolizer" decomposes it into real, color-matched bricks with stability verification. It outputs assembly animations, instruction booklets/PDFs, pricing estimates, and LDraw/CSV exports, but runs locally (two ComfyUI servers, ~16 GB VRAM NVIDIA GPU) with no hosted demo.
repos.notable: No GitHub Pages or live demo (local-only app requiring ComfyUI + NVIDIA GPU). Best portfolio material: presentation video docs/presentation/S12_charles_emilie_legoarch.mp4; two 3D-to-LEGO transformation GIFs in docs/blog/assets/ (3d-to-lego.gif, 3d-to-lego-2.gif); 11 step-by-step app UI screenshots in docs/presentation/images/views/ (prompt -> image -> mesh -> legolize -> booklet -> prices -> shelf); hero case-study montages for Bilbao/Muralla/Sagrada Familia in docs/benchmarks/assets/examples/*/(*_montage.png, *_build_app.png); brand/logo kit in docs/presentation/brand/; polished slide crops in docs/blog/assets/slide-*.png. Docs are rich: docs/architecture.md, docs/benchmarks.md, docs/concept.md, docs/design-system.md, ADR at docs/adr/0001-legolize-engine.md, plus a blog draft in docs/blog/. The 40-image LoRA training dataset (comfyui/legoarch-dataset/) and its webp copies (frontend/src/intro-assets/) are near-duplicates - sample a few at most. Repo tree was complete (truncated=false), 179 media assets total.

repos.readmeSummary: Graph-based analysis and machine-learning pipeline for architectural floor plans, trained on the Modified Swiss Dwellings dataset (Kaggle) and applied to the Narkomfin building — graph analysis (centrality, shortest paths) plus GNN node classification for room-type prediction. Team project by Charles Abi Chahine, Emilie El Chidiac, Lakzhmy Zaro, and Maria Sanchez i Dominguez.
repos.notable: Best portfolio assets are in 04_presentation/img/ — Narkomfin hero photo (narkomfin.jpg), plan graphs, exploded 3D level diagrams, and prediction-vs-ground-truth comparisons (04_presentation/img/prediction/). Repo has a local reveal-style deck at 04_presentation/index.html, but GitHub Pages is NOT enabled (github.io URL returns 404) and no live demo link exists; no video assets. Tree listing was complete (not truncated).

repos.readmeSummary: MaCAD26 Data Encoding Seminar (Group 01) project by Lakzhmy Zaro, Emilie El Chidiac, Charles Abi Chahine, and Maria Sanchez; the README itself is minimal, but the repo implements an urban street-segment risk-scoring ML pipeline (clustering, classification, SHAP explainability) for Spanish neighborhoods.
repos.notable: No GitHub Pages, index.html, screenshots folder, or live demo link found. Best portfolio visuals: per-neighborhood risk maps in output/*/*_risk_plotly.png (Eixample, Raval, Poble Sec in Barcelona; Arganzuela, Lavapies, Puente de Vallecas in Madrid; Triana in Seville) — these are the hero-image candidates. Strong supporting figures: SHAP explainability suite in plots/shap/eixample_barcelona_spain/ and Kohonen SOM heatmaps in notebooks/plots/kohonen/. Repo also has models/, notebooks/, scripts/, csv/ showing a full reproducible pipeline (KMeans, SOM, logistic regression, random forest, ablation study). README is sparse — portfolio copy will need to be written from the assets, not the README.
